turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: We'll hear arguments next Jones v. Helms. Ms. Cosgrove, you may proceed whenever you are ready.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: This case comes to the Court today on appeal from the based upon the exercise of their constitutional right to travel, and that therefore the statute is void under the Equal Protec­ tion Clause. Appellee is a father who was ordered by a Georgia court to make child support payments but who never did, who sim ply went to Alabama. He testified that he did not go to Alabama for the purpose of avoiding his child support obligations but rather to attend school. When he returned to Georgia to visit his child he was arrested on the warrant for th’e felony of abandonin Immunities Clause of the Constitution. The district court ruled against the appellee but upon appeal to the 5th Circuit the district court's order was reversed and judgment entered for appellee.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Ms. Cosgrove, I have some difficulty inter­ preting the 5th Circuit's opinion, perhaps because of its short­ ness. It seems to rely at some length on the Morissette case from this Court, which was simply as I had understood it a hand­ book of how this Court would interpret federal statutes with problem addressed by this Court. The court of appeals reasoned that inasmuch as the appellee did have a fundamental right to travel, that the statute was the subject of strict scrutiny; and secondly, since Georgia has no compelling state interest involved, that the statute would be violative of equal protec­ tion . The court of appeals also reasoned that the statute was overly broad in that we had the Uniform Reciprocal Enforce­ ment of Support Act, URESA, which was available to vindicate any jurisdiction, even without intent to avoid prosecution, he is nevertheless subject to mandatory extradition proceedings. And in addition, many states including Georgia have statutes of limitations which are tolled the instant a person leaves the state, thus dramatically increasing the time period during which a person may be punished for a crime. So, since we do not be­ lieve that there are any fundamental rights involved in this free to move to any state he wishes, stay as long as he wishes, and the statute has absolutely no impact upon him at all unless he has committed the crime of child abandonment. So this statute is unlike provisions which this Court has struck down in, for example, Crandall v. Nevada, a taxing provision that reached everyone who left the state. This stat­ ute is narrowly tailored to reach only those persons who have committed a crime.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or intend to in the course of leaving the state, or intend to abandon the child later? unfortunate situation of poverty. Rather, this statute impacts on the most blameworthy persons, those parents who abandon their helpless children. So we feel that this statute no more penalize? parents who simply wish to go to another state than does the existence of an extradition law or an indefinite statute of limitations.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, this whole problem could be solved, I suppose, if the Georgia Legislature simply made child abandonment a felony. it's Georgia's position that the statute is more than justified by our compelling interest in protecting our children and en­ forcing our criminal laws. And this interest in protecting our children appellee, does not even dispute, and it is manifested in at least two other statutes which make this same felony- For example, we have a statute which makes it a felony whereas if he stays in the state, it is a misdemeanor. And

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Not.to my understanding, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But isn't that -- that's not unheard of, though, around the country, is it?

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: There are many statutes, including federal statutes that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It could be a breach of a bail condition, I suppose.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Yes, sir, I think it could be.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about -- is that a crime in your state, contend that there was anything wrong with a felony across the board. It's simply our position that the Georgia Legislature need not make that across-the-board distinction. As I was saying, the discretion to refuse to extra­ dite for misdemeanors is totally within the purview of the has said that Georgia really does not even need to use extradi­ less drastic means, argument which the court of appeals --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you don't have to rely on it if we agree with your initial argument.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because then you're just -- need a rational basis.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. But the court of appeals used as an example of the overbreadth that we did have this alternative means available to us, and I think, I think if we were to follow the court of appeals deci­ sion in this case, we could conceivably have a situation where a person abandons his child in Georgia, goes to another juris­ diction, and even if the custodial parent -- and I might add, it is usually the custodial parent which files a URESA petition -- even if that custodial parent were successful in getting that absent parent into the court of the responding state, it is more likely than not that he could totally avoid extradition by sim­ ply agreeing to pay child support. And the difficulty with that -- you know, one might say, well, he's paying child support, that ought to settle the statute, is it? It's a statute to enforce 'the duty of support by making the parents supporters of the child?

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, and it was the court of appeals opinion that we could simply use this civil statute as a substitute for our criminal statute. It is our position, of course, that the state has a right to define and punish anti-social conduct such as child abandonment and that we have to make him think that he is going to.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Not in that particular instance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He's not guilty of this offense.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: No, he would not be guilty of child abandonment because he --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I'm telling you, he says he has aban­ doned the child.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: Well, there are two prongs, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, that's what I thought.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You don't ever catch them.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: It's very difficult, Your Honor, it really is, and I think that is one of the crucial reasons that Georgia needs this felony provision of the child abandonment statute, simply In order to be able to get this type of person back if need be. I think if we were to follow the court of appeals decision in this regard, our right to define and punish antisocial conduct such as child abandonment would be defeated because our right to enforce our laws is simply meaningless judge, Your Honor, because the jury can recommend it but the judge has to approve.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Has to do it.

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: That's correct. In sum, Your Honor, we think that the Georgia child abandonment statute, particulari V ly the felony division, is absolutely crucial to the enforce­ ment of Georgia's interests in protecting her children and in enforcing her criminal laws. We submit that the court of ap­ peals decision in this case was clearly erroneous and should be reversed. Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Bonner. here of application of the state’s police power. The state can reach fugitive parents, it can proscribe that conduct, it can reach parents who use their right to travel across a state line to frustrate the state's interest, to compound an offense. But this isn't that kind of statute, and the existence of a valid area for operation of that kind of statute don't save this one. We need to look at the -- if this were that kind of statute, then what she said about there being a reasonable connection and this being subject to the rational connection test would probably be valid. But this is not such a statute. The question before this Court as a preliminary matter It doesn't make any difference whether the man is accused before he crosses the state lines or whether he becomes accused subse­ quently, although for that subsequent class, obviously, you have that privileges and immunities problem, which we don't have any standing to raise. But it's important to look at that as­ pect of the statute because it shows what the statute does. This isn't a fugitive statute. Bobby Helms could have crossed the state line to visit his sick mother in Alabama and as long as he was subject to being charged before or after statute which applies to someone who stands accused. Bobby Helms when he left the state was not accused. At least -- the record is not clear on that, but he certainly wasn't arrested and he certainly didn't know he was accused until he came back and was arrested.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but the crime isn't committed unless he's already abandoned the child.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.name: BONNER

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It would certainly bear on the right to travel, wouldn’t it? If you left the state you've committed another misdemeanor.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Oh, I see what you mean. You're talking viously you couldn't have that kind of statute without some avoid prosecution --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE

turns.name: BONNER

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But a valid one.

turns.name: BONNER

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you insist that there be an intent requirement -- ?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: No, sir, no. All I'm saying is that in­ tent is one way to confine the intrusion on the right to

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: No problem whatsoever, Your Honor. None whatsoever. I don't think, frankly, that there would be any problem if you had a Rhode Island-type statute where it says, commits child abandonment by leaving the state. But the problem here is that you've just simply got an exercise of the right to travel which triggers it, and it's triggered even before the defendant -- He could go to Russia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But it doesn't affect it. As a matter of fact, this man traveled twice.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Well, Your Honor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, he didn't -- the first one didn't even bother.him.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: You're assuming --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Didn't he travel twice?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: You're assuming a couple things.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do I assume that he traveled twice? chilled by this statute. He's under a special jeopardy. He may want to visit --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it because he's poor?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: That's true. The poor are particularly -■

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you think a poor man would be traveling twice?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Poor and he traveled twice.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, he's poor.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: This is exactly the situation --

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Yes, sir, Justice Stevens.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm not sure I understand your position on one thing. Assume this statute said, if a person abandons a child, and thereafter, for the purpose of avoiding prosecution, leaves the state, it would be a felony; otherwise, a misdemeanor Would you say that was constitutional?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: I don't see any problem whatsoever with

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, your whole argument turns on the from this case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That’s correct.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: That's it exactly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Except that it would be after having com­ mitted an offense?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: That's what we've got here. Of course, every parent is subject, theoretically --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the reason this is a felony, not a misdemeanor, is because your client left the State of Georgia. prosecution, they could double the penalty?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Yes, sir. But that's not this kind of statute. Let me — I'm sorry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not the precise statute, but you tell me what's the distinction -- ?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: I'm sorry, Your Honor. Maybe, if I did say that, I somewhat misunderstood the hypothetical thrown at me. I think, even in that situation, you would have to have some nexus between leaving the state and intent or purpose -- ference between Bobby Helms and your hypothetical. You've got -- our situation, this particular statute, has an impact upon parents who, at the very least, are presumptively innocent of abandonment, who may not even have been charged with abandon­ ment .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but the reason they may not have been prosecuted is that they left the state and couldn't be reached.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Oh, well, Your Honor, I think later the problem of their being reached is going to dissipate. That's a child, that parent may not leave the state?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Let me run that by. I think that would -- yeah, I suppose Georgia probably could.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What they've done here is something less extreme. They've said, if you leave you get a more severe penalty. Or if a parent abandons a child, he may not leave the state. If he violates this restriction he shall be guilty of a felony.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Our problem here with this particular But you say he could do it if he escapes from prison. You'd say, well, you'd say, he can't leave the state.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: But there you've got -- one who escapes from prison knows he escaped from prison.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, by hypothesis, here, one who aban­ dons a child knows he's abandoned the child.

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Not necessarily, Your Honor. There's a great difference there. But the other thing is that at least you've got a --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he's not guilty unless he willfully

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Why are they more reasonable or less rea­ sonable than with the misdemeanor that Justice Stevens postu­ lated to you?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Well, because that's a reasonable intru­ sion onto the right to travel, vis-a-vis the state's interest in having him there and prosecuting him. I don't1really see any analogy there. But this, again, is the same situation as if Georgia had isolated, say, the right to trial by jury and made that there a: special penalty of death, as in Jackson v.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, I know, but no part of your case ques­ tions at all the authority of the state to criminalize?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: No, sir, I think there are a lot of policy reasons why not, and I think that's why you've got URESA, because of the judgments that the state made that this kind of problem is better approached civilly.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You agree that the jury could have made it a misdemeanor?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Yes, but that's just entirely discre­ state interest. The state basically, their interest here comes down to extradition. When you look behind everything they can say about the welfare of the child and about the enforcement of the criminal law, on both avenues it boils down to aiding' extradition. But the only -- the thing is that that's not neces­ sarily compelling interest because we've got the Extradition Act, we've got URESA, we've got uniform reciprocal -- whatever would still encounter those relief provisions that the state talked about. A judge in Alabama could still -- or, a judge in another state, by entertaining an action brought by the father over there, could completely bar extradition under URESA, regardless of whether it's a felony or misdemeanor. But in any event URESA is designed to be a supplemen­ tary kind of extradition procedure, not one that necessarily preempts. I forget exactly what section of URESA it is, but the state's perfectly free to proceed under all its other extra­ dition powers. And again, under all of those extradition powers around so that support may be collected?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Oh, maybe I should back up. Nothing would be wrong with that. That would be --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, I know. Why isn't that a compelling interest?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Well, that might be how the Chief Jus­ tice's question a minute ago makes sense about one under indict­ ment can't leave the state. I'm sorry. I'm sure If anyone's not making sense that it comes from here, not from there. No, I mean, that's how his example is explicable, because, you dependent minor children behind them?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: I don't think the state could.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what's 'the statute ex cent thdt? That's what this statute is, isn't it?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: That's exactly why the statute is wrong.

turns.text: Yes, but you say they1 can't --

turns.name: BONNER

turns.text: That's your reason for saying it's bad?

turns.name: BONNER
turns.text: Yes, sir. That Georgia couldn't do that. This is a different degree of intrusion, but it's exactly the same kind of pattern of intrusion. Here, essentially, Georgia I'll sit down.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Do you have anything fur­ ther?

turns.name: CAROL ATHA COSGROVE
turns.text: I don't have anything further.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE BURGER
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
